failure_surface: Complex nested INDEX formula written to cells did not evaluate correctly in Excel, resulting in test failures despite code execution completing successfully.
root_cause: The agent constructed an incorrectly structured nested INDEX formula using invalid 2D range indexing (INDEX with '0' as row argument on multi-column ranges). The formula appeared syntactically valid to Python but fails during Excel formula evaluation because INDEX() cannot accept a 2D range with zero-based row/column indices in this manner.
minimal_fix_hypothesis: Before writing complex formulas to cells, verify the formula string matches valid Excel syntax for the target spreadsheet engine, particularly regarding 2D range handling with INDEX(). Use simpler LOOKUP-style patterns instead of deeply nested INDEX calls when cross-referencing data across sheets.
evidence: Agent used formula: INDEX(INDEX(Sheet2!$C:$M,0,MATCH($C$5,...)),MATCH(...),INDEX(...),0) which has nested INDEX calls with '0' row/column arguments on 2D ranges
Test results show eval.test_case_results: [0] indicating complete failure despite code completion logs showing '✓ Successfully updated formulas'
The inner INDEX with range Sheet2!$C:$M and row=0 does not correspond to any valid Excel INDEX usage pattern for 2D selection
Agent verified by reading back the formula string via openpyxl, not by checking formula evaluation in Excel context

memory_items.title: Validate Complex Formula Syntax Before Writing
memory_items.lesson: When constructing complex INDEX/MATCH formulas referencing multiple sheets and columns, ensure the formula follows valid Excel syntax rules for 2D range operations.
memory_items.generalization: Nested INDEX calls with multi-cell ranges require proper row/column index arguments; using '0' as a dimension specifier may compile in code but fail during Excel evaluation.

memory_items.title: Prefer Simpler Formula Patterns for Cross-Sheet Lookups
memory_items.lesson: For lookup tasks involving dynamic column selection based on header matching, consider SUMPRODUCT-based or HLOOKUP-style approaches over deeply nested INDEX constructions.
memory_items.generalization: Simpler formula structures reduce evaluation errors and are easier to debug when working with external spreadsheets through programmatic interfaces.

memory_items.title: Verify Formula Evaluation Beyond String Presence
memory_items.lesson: Reading back the formula text via cell.value confirms writing succeeded but does not confirm the formula evaluates correctly in Excel.
memory_items.generalization: After writing formulas programmatically, validate by opening the workbook in an Excel-compatible viewer or checking calculated results, not just formula strings.

edits.content:
## 6. Formula Construction & Validation
- **Complex Formulas**: When creating nested INDEX/MATCH lookups spanning multiple worksheets, prefer simpler lookup patterns (e.g., SUMPRODUCT, HLOOKUP) over deeply nested constructs.
- **2D Range Handling**: Avoid using INDEX() with multi-column ranges and zero-based dimension arguments unless explicitly supported by the target spreadsheet engine.
- **Formula Verification**: After writing formulas to cells, verify they evaluate correctly by checking computed results, not just confirming formula strings were stored.
